ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general squid food product class; no specific grilled whole squid with garlic butter entry exists. closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CJW covers squid and cuttlefish as a category; no specific grilled preparation with garlic butter exists at a more granular level.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226888001 represents squid as a food item; no specific grilled whole squid with garlic butter concept exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: One serving is approximately one whole grilled squid weighing around 250 g, as typically served at a seafood restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Squid, mixed species, cooked, moist heat — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15150)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base nutrient values derived from USDA SR Legacy cooked squid (NDB 15150). Garlic butter addition estimated to contribute approximately 2 g fat and 18 kcal per 100 g of finished dish based on USDA butter and garlic data (FDC 173410, FDC 169230). Final per-100g values represent the composite grilled squid with garlic butter dish. Sodium adjusted upward to reflect seasoning typical of restaurant preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for grilled whole squid with garlic butter. Squid and other lean seafood are very low in digestible carbohydrates (1.8 g per 100 g). GI estimated at 40 by analogy with other low-carbohydrate cooked seafood and shellfish reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 and the University of Sydney GI Database, which consistently place lean seafood in the low-GI category (GI 30–50). Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (40 × 1.8 × 2.5) / 100 = 1.8 g, confirming negligible glycemic impact.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from seasoning and garlic butter
hypertension.guidance: Each 250 g serving provides approximately 800 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should request reduced seasoning or limit portion size to manage daily sodium intake below 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 1.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g and a GI of approximately 40, grilled squid has a negligible glycemic load and is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-carbohydrate accompaniments.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High cholesterol content offset by lean protein and omega-3 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Squid is high in dietary cholesterol (approximately 233 mg per 100 g) but low in saturated fat; individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size and be aware that the garlic butter addition increases saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grilled Whole Squid with Garlic Butter is a popular seafood dish served at Chinese and Singaporean seafood restaurants, featuring a whole squid that is cleaned, scored, and grilled over high heat until lightly charred, then basted or finished with a garlic-infused butter sauce. The dish delivers a smoky, savoury flavour with a tender yet slightly chewy texture characteristic of well-cooked squid. The garlic butter adds richness and aromatic depth. Nutritionally, squid is a lean, high-protein seafood providing approximately 17.9 g protein per 100 g with only 3.8 g fat, making it a relatively low-calorie option at around 110 kcal per 100 g. It is an excellent source of vitamin B12, selenium, and phosphorus. The carbohydrate content is very low at 1.8 g per 100 g, resulting in a negligible glycemic load suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium content is moderate to high due to restaurant seasoning, which is a consideration for those with hypertension. The dish is commonly served as a shared starter or main course at zi char stalls and seafood restaurants in Singapore, often accompanied by steamed rice or vegetables.
common_user_queries: How many calories in grilled whole squid?
Is grilled squid high in protein?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked squid with estimated adjustment for garlic butter addition. No exact Singapore restaurant-specific nutrient analysis exists for this dish. GI is an analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate seafood category data from peer-reviewed literature.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy squid entry and no reliable value is available for this preparation. Trans fat is 0.0 confirmed as naturally zero for plain grilled squid; trace amounts from butter are below detection threshold and reported as 0.0. Dietary fibre is 0.0 as squid contains no plant-based fibre; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. Sodium value of 320 mg per 100 g reflects estimated restaurant-level seasoning; the USDA base value for cooked squid is approximately 260 mg per 100 g and has been adjusted upward to account for garlic butter and salt used in restaurant preparation. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. Halal status marked false as butter of unspecified origin is used; dish may be halal-certifiable if halal-certified butter is used. Price data from field survey data (SGD 31.90) retained as contextual metadata from source record.
